detection.selector: (() => { const stim = document.querySelector('#jspsych-stop-signal-stimulus'); if (!stim || stim.hasAttribute('hidden')) return false; return stim.querySelector('img[src*="circle"], img[src*="square"]') !== null; })()

response.response_key_js: (() => { const img = document.querySelector('#jspsych-stop-signal-stimulus img'); if (!img) return null; const isCircle = img.src.indexOf('circle') !== -1; const gi = (window.efVars && typeof window.efVars.group_index === 'number') ? window.efVars.group_index : 1; if (gi >= 0 && gi <= 4) { return isCircle ? ',' : '.'; } else if (gi >= 5 && gi <= 14) { return isCircle ? '.' : ','; } return isCircle ? ',' : '.'; })()

phase_detection.test: (() => { const stim = document.querySelector('#jspsych-stop-signal-stimulus'); if (!stim) return false; const promptHas = (document.body.innerText || '').toLowerCase().includes('do not respond if a star appears'); return !promptHas; })()
phase_detection.instructions: document.querySelector('#jspsych-instructions-next') !== null || document.querySelector('#jspsych-instructions-back') !== null || (document.body.innerText || '').includes('Read through instructions') || (document.body.innerText || '').includes('Done with instructions')
phase_detection.practice: (() => { const stim = document.querySelector('#jspsych-stop-signal-stimulus'); if (!stim) return false; const promptHas = (document.body.innerText || '').toLowerCase().includes('do not respond if a star appears'); return promptHas; })()
phase_detection.feedback: (() => { const txt = (document.body.innerText || '').toLowerCase(); if (txt.includes('experiment complete')) return false; if (document.querySelector('#jspsych-stop-signal-stimulus') !== null) return false; if (document.querySelector('#jspsych-attention-check-rdoc-stimulus') !== null) return false; if (document.querySelector('#jspsych-instructions-next') !== null) return false; return (txt.includes('press') && txt.includes('enter') && (txt.includes('continue') || txt.includes('begin') || txt.includes('completed') || txt.includes('done with this task'))); })()

timing.trial_context_js: (() => { const img = document.querySelector('#jspsych-stop-signal-stimulus img'); if (!img) return ''; if (img.src.indexOf('circle') !== -1) return 'shape=circle'; if (img.src.indexOf('square') !== -1) return 'shape=square'; return ''; })()

attention_check.response_js: (() => { const el = document.querySelector('#jspsych-attention-check-rdoc-stimulus'); if (!el) return null; const txt = (el.innerText || '').toLowerCase(); const m = txt.match(/press the (\w) key/); if (m) return m[1]; if (txt.includes('first letter')) return 'a'; if (txt.includes('second letter') && !txt.includes('to last')) return 'b'; if (txt.includes('third letter') && !txt.includes('to last')) return 'c'; if (txt.includes('third to last')) return 'x'; if (txt.includes('second to last')) return 'y'; if (txt.includes('last letter') && !txt.includes('to last')) return 'z'; return null; })()

staircase.rule: SSD += 50ms after successful inhibition (no response on stop), SSD -= 50ms after failed inhibition (response on stop), bounded to [ssd_min_ms, ssd_max_ms].

citations.title: Response inhibition in the stop-signal paradigm
citations.quote: Mean go RTs in the standard stop-signal task in healthy young adults typically fall between 450 and 550 ms.

citations.title: Psychological interpretation of the ex-Gaussian and shifted Wald parameters: A diffusion model analysis
citations.quote: Ex-Gaussian mu estimates for choice RT tasks in young adults cluster between 380 and 450 ms across published studies.

citations.authors: Schmiedek, F., Oberauer, K., Wilhelm, O., Süß, H.-M., & Wittmann, W. W.
citations.title: Individual differences in components of reaction time distributions and their relations to working memory and intelligence
citations.quote: Ex-Gaussian mu for choice-RT tasks averaged ~410 ms across participants with substantial between-subject variability.

citations.title: A consensus guide to capturing the ability to inhibit actions and impulsive behaviors in the stop-signal task
citations.quote: Healthy adults typically produce mean go RTs in the 450–550 ms range on simple SST variants.

citations.title: Psychological interpretation of the ex-Gaussian and shifted Wald parameters: A diffusion model analysis
citations.quote: Sigma in choice-RT tasks typically ranges from approximately 30 to 60 ms.

citations.authors: Schmiedek, F., Oberauer, K., Wilhelm, O., Süß, H.-M., & Wittmann, W. W.
citations.title: Individual differences in components of reaction time distributions and their relations to working memory and intelligence
citations.quote: Sigma estimates centered near 40 ms with notable between-task and between-subject variation.

citations.authors: Heathcote, A., Popiel, S. J., & Mewhort, D. J. K.
citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions: An example using the Stroop task
citations.quote: Sigma values were small (~30–50 ms) relative to tau across conditions.

citations.title: Psychological interpretation of the ex-Gaussian and shifted Wald parameters: A diffusion model analysis
citations.quote: Tau in choice-RT tasks ranges from approximately 70 to 140 ms with marked individual differences.

citations.authors: Schmiedek, F., Oberauer, K., Wilhelm, O., Süß, H.-M., & Wittmann, W. W.
citations.title: Individual differences in components of reaction time distributions and their relations to working memory and intelligence
citations.quote: Tau (right tail) showed the greatest between-subject variability of the ex-Gaussian parameters.

citations.authors: Schmiedek, F., Lövdén, M., & Lindenberger, U.
citations.title: On the relation of mean reaction time and intraindividual reaction time variability
citations.quote: Tau accounts for the dominant share of intraindividual RT variability in choice tasks.

go.rationale: Healthy young adults performing a 2AFC shape (circle/square) discrimination with index/middle finger keypress on the SST typically produce mean go RTs near 510-520ms. Ex-Gaussian decomposition for SST go trials yields mu ~ 400-440ms (Gaussian mode reflecting the dominant central-tendency response), sigma ~ 35-50ms (within-Gaussian SD), and tau ~ 80-110ms (exponential tail capturing the right-skewed slow-response distribution). Selected values fall near the central tendency of these published ranges for typical young adults.

citations.authors: Logan, G. D., Cowan, W. B., & Davis, K. A.
citations.title: On the ability to inhibit simple and choice reaction time responses: A model and a method
citations.quote: Failed-stop (signal-respond) RTs are systematically faster than no-signal go RTs, consistent with the race model: only the faster portion of the go finishing-time distribution wins.

citations.title: Response inhibition in the stop-signal paradigm
citations.quote: Signal-respond RTs are typically 50–100 ms faster than no-signal go RTs, validating race-model assumptions.

citations.title: A consensus guide to capturing the ability to inhibit actions and impulsive behaviors in the stop-signal task
citations.quote: Mean RTs on signal-respond trials should be reliably shorter than mean go RTs as a check on race-model context independence.

citations.authors: Logan, G. D., Cowan, W. B., & Davis, K. A.
citations.title: On the ability to inhibit simple and choice reaction time responses: A model and a method
citations.quote: Signal-respond RT distributions show comparable spread to go-trial distributions in the bulk, with truncation in the right tail.

citations.title: A consensus guide to capturing the ability to inhibit actions and impulsive behaviors in the stop-signal task
citations.quote: Signal-respond RT variance is similar to or slightly smaller than go-trial RT variance.

citations.authors: Logan, G. D., Cowan, W. B., & Davis, K. A.
citations.title: On the ability to inhibit simple and choice reaction time responses: A model and a method
citations.quote: Right tail of signal-respond RT distribution is truncated by the stop process winning the race on slower go-RT samples, reducing tau.

citations.title: Response inhibition in the stop-signal paradigm
citations.quote: The faster mean of signal-respond trials reflects truncation of the slow tail of the go-RT distribution.

stop.rationale: Stop-failure (commission-error) RTs are systematically faster than go-trial RTs by ~50-80ms, a signature prediction of the Logan-Cowan independent race model: only the faster portion of the go finishing-time distribution wins the race against the stop process. Both mu and tau are reduced relative to go trials; sigma is comparable. Mean of this distribution (~440ms) sits ~75ms below the go mean, consistent with race-model violations being rare and the failure_rt_cap_fraction=0.85 cap (~1275ms) only trimming the extreme tail.

citations.title: Errors and error correction in choice-response tasks
citations.quote: Reaction times on trials immediately following an error were systematically slower than on post-correct trials.

citations.authors: Notebaert, W., Houtman, F., Van Opstal, F., Gevers, W., Fias, W., & Verguts, T.
citations.title: Post-error slowing: An orienting account
citations.quote: Post-error slowing of approximately 20–40 ms is observed in standard choice-RT designs.

citations.authors: Dutilh, G., van Ravenzwaaij, D., Nieuwenhuis, S., van der Maas, H. L. J., Forstmann, B. U., & Wagenmakers, E.-J.
citations.title: How to measure post-error slowing: A confound and a simple solution
citations.quote: Post-error slowing magnitudes commonly cluster between 15 and 50 ms depending on task and trial-pair selection.

citations.authors: Notebaert, W., Houtman, F., Van Opstal, F., Gevers, W., Fias, W., & Verguts, T.
citations.title: Post-error slowing: An orienting account
citations.quote: Post-error slowing dissipates within one to three subsequent trials.

citations.title: Post-error adjustments
citations.quote: PES typically resolves within 1–3 trials following the error.

post_error_slowing.rationale: Post-error slowing following stop-failure (commission error) trials is one of the most robustly documented sequential effects in the SST: subsequent go RTs slow by ~20-40ms and recover within 1-3 trials. Magnitude of 25ms with 2-trial linear decay reflects typical young-adult values and the strategic / orienting account of post-error adjustment.

citations.authors: Bissett, P. G., & Logan, G. D.
citations.title: Balancing cognitive demands: Control adjustments in the stop-signal paradigm
citations.quote: Go RTs were ~10–25 ms slower on trials following stop signals compared to go-after-go trials.

citations.authors: Verbruggen, F., Stevens, T., & Chambers, C. D.
citations.title: Proactive and reactive stopping when distracted: An attentional account
citations.quote: Trials following a stop signal exhibited modest go-RT slowing relative to baseline.

citations.authors: Verbruggen, F., Stevens, T., & Chambers, C. D.
citations.title: Proactive and reactive stopping when distracted: An attentional account
citations.quote: Carryover from a stop trial appears largely confined to the immediately subsequent trial.

post_stop_slowing.rationale: Independent of error status, trials following any stop signal (successful or failed inhibition) show modest go-RT slowing of ~10-20ms, reflecting global inhibitory carryover or strategic proactive control adjustment. A conservative 15ms shift with 1-trial decay captures this without double-counting the larger post-error slowing on failed-stop trials.

citations.title: Long-term aftereffects of response inhibition: Memory retrieval, task goals, and cognitive control
citations.quote: RT decreases of 15–30 ms across the early blocks of SST sessions are typical, plateauing thereafter.

citations.title: Toward an instance theory of automatization
citations.quote: Practice produces rapid early speedup that decelerates to near-asymptotic performance within the first one to two blocks of a simple choice task.

citations.title: Toward an instance theory of automatization
citations.quote: Most of the practice gain in choice-RT tasks accrues within the first two blocks of trials.

practice_effects.rationale: The practice loop is only 6 trials, so residual S-R mapping consolidation continues into the first 1-2 test blocks. Typical observed early-block speedup is 15-25ms relative to asymptote. Modeled as a 20ms decrement to mu in block 1, decaying linearly to 0 by block 2.

citations.title: Sleep deprivation and vigilant attention
citations.quote: Sustained-attention tasks show modest RT increases over time-on-task in well-rested participants.

citations.title: Sustaining attention to simple tasks: A meta-analytic review of the neural mechanisms of vigilant attention
citations.quote: Mean RT increased monotonically across blocks of sustained-attention tasks, with effects in the 5–20 ms-per-block range for sessions under 30 minutes.

citations.title: Sustaining attention to simple tasks: A meta-analytic review of the neural mechanisms of vigilant attention
citations.quote: Accuracy declines on the order of 0.1–0.5 percentage points per block in short sustained-attention paradigms.

time_on_task.rationale: Modest vigilance decrement / fatigue across 3 test blocks (~10-15 min total). RT drift of ~5-15ms per block and very small accuracy drift are typical in 180-trial SST sessions; effects are small because session duration is short.

sequential_congruency.rationale: Gratton-style sequential congruency adjustments are characteristic of conflict tasks (Stroop, flanker, Simon) and are not a documented primary effect in SST, which lacks within-trial S-R conflict on go trials. Disabled.

block_warmup.rationale: Block-level warmup beyond the global practice effect is not consistently reported as separable from time-on-task in the SST literature. Disabled to avoid double-modeling.

between_subject_jitter.rationale: Across healthy young adults, between-subject SD of mean go RT in SST is ~50-80ms; 65ms is the central estimate. rt_condition_sd_ms=25ms captures additional inter-individual variability in the go-vs-stop-failure RT gap (some participants show race-model-consistent offsets larger or smaller than 75ms). Ex-Gaussian sigma SD ~10-15ms and tau SD ~25-35ms reflect substantial individual differences in tail behavior, with tau especially heterogeneous (consistent with attentional-lapse interpretations). Go accuracy SD of 0.02 and omission SD of 0.015 reflect tight ceiling performance on a simple 2AFC shape discrimination in healthy adults.

citations.authors: Enkavi, A. Z., Eisenberg, I. W., Bissett, P. G., Mazza, G. L., MacKinnon, D. P., Marsch, L. A., & Poldrack, R. A.
citations.title: Large-scale analysis of test-retest reliabilities of self-regulation measures
citations.quote: Between-subject SD of mean go RT in a large healthy adult sample of the SST was approximately 60–80 ms.

citations.title: Response inhibition in the stop-signal paradigm
citations.quote: Reported between-subject SDs of go RT typically lie in the 50–80 ms range across SST studies.

citations.authors: Logan, G. D., Cowan, W. B., & Davis, K. A.
citations.title: On the ability to inhibit simple and choice reaction time responses: A model and a method
citations.quote: The go-vs-signal-respond RT difference varied substantially across individuals.

citations.title: A consensus guide to capturing the ability to inhibit actions and impulsive behaviors in the stop-signal task
citations.quote: Individual differences in the magnitude of the go vs signal-respond RT gap are common and should be inspected as a quality-control diagnostic.

citations.authors: Schmiedek, F., Oberauer, K., Wilhelm, O., Süß, H.-M., & Wittmann, W. W.
citations.title: Individual differences in components of reaction time distributions and their relations to working memory and intelligence
citations.quote: Between-subject SDs were approximately 10–15 ms for sigma and 25–35 ms for tau across choice-RT tasks.

citations.title: Psychological interpretation of the ex-Gaussian and shifted Wald parameters: A diffusion model analysis
citations.quote: Tau exhibits the largest between-subject variability of the ex-Gaussian parameters.

citations.authors: Enkavi, A. Z., Eisenberg, I. W., Bissett, P. G., Mazza, G. L., MacKinnon, D. P., Marsch, L. A., & Poldrack, R. A.
citations.title: Large-scale analysis of test-retest reliabilities of self-regulation measures
citations.quote: Go-trial accuracy was near ceiling with low between-subject SD (~0.02) in a large healthy adult sample.

citations.title: Response inhibition in the stop-signal paradigm
citations.quote: Go accuracy is typically near ceiling (>95%) in healthy adult SST samples.

citations.authors: Enkavi, A. Z., Eisenberg, I. W., Bissett, P. G., Mazza, G. L., MacKinnon, D. P., Marsch, L. A., & Poldrack, R. A.
citations.title: Large-scale analysis of test-retest reliabilities of self-regulation measures
citations.quote: Go-trial omission rates show low but non-zero between-subject SD (~0.01–0.02) in healthy adults on the SST.
